did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study performed external validation of the 'ProRodinki' medical device, which utilizes an artificial intelligence algorithm (based on neural networks) for classifying pigmented and amelanotic skin lesions to assess malignancy risk. The AI method was used as the index test to obtain diagnostic results (sensitivity 86%, specificity 41%) in a dermatology context.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'Original research' (Оригинальные исследования) in the header. It describes a prospective external validation study involving 132 patients, with specific methods for patient selection, data collection (photography, histology), and statistical analysis to evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of the 'ProRodinki' medical device.

title: Diagnostic accuracy of the ProRodinki medical device for basal cell carcinoma
brief_summary: This study conducted an external prospective validation of the 'ProRodinki' medical device, an artificial intelligence algorithm based on neural networks, for diagnosing basal cell carcinoma. The research involved 132 patients with pigmented and amelanotic skin lesions, using histological and cytological results as the reference standard and the app's classification as the index test. The algorithm demonstrated a high sensitivity of 86% (95% CI: 78–92%) and a positive predictive value of 88%, but a low specificity of 41% (95% CI: 21–64%). The authors concluded that while the tool shows potential for screening due to its high sensitivity, its specificity is unsatisfactory. They recommended retraining the model with sampling and hyperparameter optimization, as well as implementing automatic image quality assessment to improve diagnostic accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the diagnostic accuracy study. Confirmed in 110 out of 132 patients via histological or cytological reference standard.

all_task_types.target: Malignancy risk (Basal Cell Carcinoma vs. Benign neoplasms)
all_task_types.details: Binary classification of pigmented and amelanotic skin lesions into malignant (basal cell carcinoma) or benign categories using the 'ProRodinki' AI algorithm based on neural networks. The study evaluates diagnostic accuracy metrics (sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV) against histological and cytological reference standards.

datasets_used.name: ProRodinki External Validation Cohort
datasets_used.description: A prospective external validation cohort of 132 patients with pigmented and amelanotic skin lesions, recruited from the Oncology Center No. 1 of the S.S. Yudin City Clinical Hospital in Moscow, Russia, between August 15, 2024, and January 20, 2025. The dataset consists of smartphone photographs of skin lesions taken by physicians and corresponding histological or cytological reference results.

use.testing: The dataset was used as the index test evaluation set to assess the diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV) of the 'ProRodinki' AI medical device against histological/cytological reference standards.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the uncertainty of diagnostic accuracy metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, PPV, NPV).
To calculate 95% confidence intervals for diagnostic metrics, specifically accounting for the risk of small subgroup sizes.
To compare the index test (ProRodinki app) results against the reference standard (histological and cytological findings) for calculating diagnostic accuracy metrics.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Statistical analysis was limited to calculating diagnostic accuracy metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, PPV, NPV) with 95% confidence intervals using the Clopper-Pearson method.

preprocessing_techniques: Automatic image quality assessment (suggested for future implementation to exclude unsatisfactory quality images, but not reported as applied in this study)

No explicit preprocessing techniques (e.g., resizing, cropping, normalization, denoising) were reported; the proprietary 'ProRodinki' algorithm processed raw smartphone photographs directly, including those with unsatisfactory quality (out of focus, blurred) in 75% of cases
conclusions: The 'ProRodinki' AI medical device demonstrated high sensitivity (86%) for diagnosing basal cell carcinoma, making it potentially suitable for screening purposes.
The device exhibited low specificity (41%), which the authors deemed unsatisfactory for definitive diagnosis without further refinement.
Image quality was a significant limitation, with 75% of smartphone photographs being of unsatisfactory quality (out of focus or blurred), which likely impacted diagnostic performance.
To improve specificity, the authors recommend retraining the neural network model using sampling techniques, hyperparameter optimization, and data dimensionality reduction.
The implementation of automatic image quality assessment is recommended to exclude unsatisfactory images and improve overall diagnostic accuracy.
The study highlights the critical importance of external prospective validation for AI algorithms in dermatology, as many existing algorithms lack robust evidence bases.
important_remarks: The study highlights a significant limitation in image quality, with 75% of smartphone photographs being of unsatisfactory quality (out of focus or blurred), which likely contributed to the low specificity (41%) and may have influenced the diagnostic performance.

The algorithm demonstrated high sensitivity (86%) but unsatisfactory specificity (41%), leading the authors to conclude that while suitable for screening, it requires refinement for definitive diagnosis.
The authors recommend retraining the neural network model using sampling techniques, hyperparameter optimization, and data dimensionality reduction to improve specificity.

The study emphasizes the critical need for external prospective validation of AI algorithms in dermatology, noting that many existing algorithms lack robust evidence bases due to reliance on retrospective data.

There is a discrepancy in reported performance compared to a previous validation by N.M. Blokhin National Medical Research Center of Oncology (NMIC of Oncology); the authors note that access to the NMIC document is restricted, making it difficult to determine the cause of the difference in results.
The study population consisted of patients from a single oncology center in Moscow, Russia, which may limit the generalizability of the results to other populations or clinical settings.

Physicians involved in the study had no prior experience with the 'ProRodinki' application before the study began, which may have contributed to the poor quality of the captured images and suggests a need for standardized protocols and training.
The study utilized both histological and cytological results as the reference standard, with a total of 132 patients included (110 with basal cell carcinoma and 22 with benign neoplasms).
